## Summary of Findings [PLACEHOLDER]
<!-- [PLACEHOLDER] -->

The thesis set out to answer three hypotheses concerning deep reinforcement learning
for high-frequency single-asset trading using order-book-derived features. This
section summarizes the empirical evidence for each.

**Hypothesis 1: the TD3 agent can outperform benchmark strategies on a
risk-adjusted basis.** The evaluation supports this hypothesis within the
scope of the controlled experiment. The trained TD3 agent achieved positive
test-set risk-adjusted returns and outperformed the evaluated benchmark
strategies on both the Sharpe ratio and realized cumulative return dimensions,
with this edge persisting after accounting for position-change
turnover in the zero-fee baseline setting. The agent's behavior was also
economically coherent: position sizing varied with the state of the order book
rather than producing constant directional exposure, and drawdown during the
test period remained contained relative to the buy-and-hold benchmark. These
results should be read as a proof of concept rather than a
generalization claim: the test split is drawn from the same three-day market
episode as the training data, and "out-of-sample" here means held-out within
that window, not evaluated across different market conditions. The finding
establishes that the learning problem is tractable and that the chosen feature
set carries sufficient signal for the agent to develop a policy superior to
passive benchmarks under these conditions.

**Hypothesis 2: a broader microstructure-aware feature set improves out-of-sample
performance relative to a simpler snapshot-based state representation.** The
feature-set comparison confirms this hypothesis. The extended state
representation — which supplements basic top-of-book snapshot quantities with
order flow imbalance, spread regime features, book shape measures, and temporal
encodings — produced superior out-of-sample risk-adjusted returns compared to
the baseline feature set under identical training and evaluation conditions. The
improvement was visible not only in summary metrics but in policy behavior: the
richer state representation supported lower-turnover, more stable positioning
that translated into better realized returns net of effective execution costs.
This confirms that short-horizon price movement signal is concentrated in LOB
structure, and that making it available to the agent yields a measurably better
policy.

**Hypothesis 3: empirical performance is materially sensitive to feature
specification, reward design, and transaction-cost assumptions.** The
robustness analysis confirms this hypothesis as well, and in doing so it
clarifies what drives the performance advantage described above. Feature
specification was the dominant determinant of policy quality: configurations
using only snapshot-level quantities produced weaker and less stable policies
than those using the full microstructure-aware feature set. The choice of reward
function also mattered: the Differential Sharpe Ratio reward produced more
stable training behavior than the raw log-return baseline, consistent with
the theoretical argument that direct risk-adjusted optimization improves
sample-to-sample stability. Transaction-cost sensitivity testing further
confirmed that the agent's edge is meaningful at low but not at high transaction
cost levels, which appropriately bounds the strategy's viability and underscores
the importance of realistic cost modeling in any empirical assessment of
this type.
